system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — beginners-guide

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

This book teaches jazz guitar in a deliberate sequence that moves from harmonic foundation to rhythmic application to melodic improvisation, then grounds everything in real repertoire before systematizing technique. Chapter 1 establishes the entire harmonic vocabulary as a moveable-root system—five chord qualities anchored to the ii–V–I–vi progression—while simultaneously introducing the rhythmic comping figures (Freddie Green, anticipation, Charleston) that will govern accompaniment throughout. Chapter 2 immediately applies that foundation to five canonical jazz standards, establishing the book's depth-over-breadth principle and its ear-first methodology (listen to recordings before touching the fretboard). Chapters 3 and 4 then systematize the soloing tools: arpeggios in Chapter 3 as broken-chord melodic devices independent of comping shapes, and scales in Chapter 4 as harmonically-assigned color sources deployed by the rule "when the chords change, you change." Chapter 5 extends this into a lick vocabulary that names specific bebop and blues techniques, and Chapter 6 unifies everything in the jazz blues form as a harmonic and expressive proving ground. The Roman numeral appendix (Chapter 7) supplies the notational infrastructure—capital vs. small numerals encoding chord quality—that the book's chord charts presuppose.

## The Master's Characteristic Systems and Terminology

The book operates on two interlocking systems: a moveable-root harmonic system and a function-assigned scale/arpeggio system. In the first, every chord shape is defined by a red dot at the root, so the chord's name follows its physical position on the neck—the transposition rule is "root = chord name." In the second, each scale or arpeggio is assigned to a specific harmonic function rather than to a key center, so harmonic minor belongs to the tonic when its V7 is altered, melodic minor belongs to the m7 chord as expressive tension, and so on. These two systems are complementary: the first governs comping and chord knowledge, the second governs improvisation choices.

The book's load-bearing terminology clusters around three domains. Harmonic vocabulary: maj7, dominant 7, m7, half-diminished 7, diminished 7 as the five essential qualities; ii–V–I as the master progression; tritone substitution (a 7th chord a tritone away preserving the 3rd and 7th); Bird blues (Imaj7 tonic); quick-change blues; V7alt (altered dominant); turnaround substitutions (bVI–V–I, ii–V–I, biii–bVI/ii–V–I in minor blues). Melodic/soloing vocabulary: approach notes (one fret below, resolving to chord tone); enclosures (surrounding a target note with tones one fret above and below); the 3-to-9 arpeggio (diminished 7 from the 3rd of any 7th chord); side-stepping (alternating minor pentatonic scales a half step apart); the phrygian dominant scale (fifth mode of harmonic minor, for V7 chords); blue notes (b3 and b5); the major blues scale (major pentatonic plus b3). Rhythmic vocabulary: anticipation (placing the next bar's chord on the & of 4); the Freddie Green rhythm (quarter notes with 2-and-4 accents); the Charleston pattern; syncopation discipline requiring metronome practice.

## Rules and Prescriptions the Book Establishes

Several explicit rules recur across chapters and carry the weight of prescriptions rather than suggestions:

- Root = chord name: every moveable shape is named by the red-dot root's position, making transposition automatic.
- Ear first: listen to recordings before working anything on the fretboard.
- Depth over breadth: thoroughly master each standard or technique before moving on.
- When the chords change, you change (Joe Pass's rule): scale selection is chord-specific, not key-global.
- Voice-lead by proximity: connect arpeggios by nearness of pitches rather than restarting from the root each time.
- Arpeggios are free to start anywhere: any note of the arpeggio may begin the phrase; ordering is creative, not fixed.
- Major blues scale avoids clash by lacking a 7th, making it usable over both dominant 7 and major 7 chords without collision.
- Blue notes define blues feeling: b3 and b5 are not errors or passing tones but the essential expressive content.
- Metronome for syncopation: any syncopated comping figure must be practiced with a metronome.
- Phrase-by-phrase for repertoire: learn standards section by section, not in a single pass.
- Extract concepts from licks, do not parrot them: the goal of lick study is transferable vocabulary, not imitation.

## Explicit Methods and Methodology

The book teaches three identifiable methods with stepwise structure.

The chord-melody method (Chapter 2) specifies three simultaneous elements: bass notes on lower strings, chord voicings in the middle register, and melody confined to the upper strings. This is presented as the integration of comping and soloing into a single-instrument arrangement technique.

The arpeggio improvisation method (Chapter 3) begins by detaching arpeggios from chord shapes (they are played with plectrum, muted to prevent ringing), then assigns five moveable grips corresponding to the CAGED system, then specifies three functional uses in improvisation—outlining harmonic direction, enabling smoother voice leading, and adding color through substitutions—and finally liberates the player to start on any arpeggio tone and decorate with approach notes and enclosures.

The blues solo practice method (Chapter 6) is explicitly three-staged: (1) imitation—transcribe and reproduce recorded solos; (2) composition—write out solo lines before playing them; (3) ear training—internalize melodic choices so they arise spontaneously. This sequence parallels the broader book arc from imitation (learning licks and standards) through application (assigned scales and arpeggios) to internalization (blues as expressive proving ground).

Two named masters provide exemplar moves that the book treats as teachable templates: Charlie Christian's enclosures, lower neighbor tones after leaps, and the b3–3–5 riff mixing blue notes with diatonic intervals; and Joe Pass's chord-melody rhythm technique of splitting bass from treble with selective accent, and his minor/major pentatonic mixing over jazz blues. Kenny Burrell's chord/single-note mixing in Chapter 6 is a third named approach. These are not biographical references but technique templates the student is expected to absorb and deploy.

# Chapter 1 — Jazz Guitar Chords

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This chapter teaches the five essential jazz chord qualities (maj7, dominant 7, minor 7, half diminished 7, diminished 7) using a moveable root-position system where the red dot indicates the chord's root and determines its name, combined with specific right-hand fingerpicking technique (thumb on bass, fingers 1-3 on upper voices) and the foundational ii–V–I–vi progression drawn from the major scale. The master's characteristic pedagogical moves appear in the transposition rule ("root = chord name") and the systematic progression from steady comping rhythms through the Freddie Green rhythm (quarter notes with 2-and-4 accents) to more sophisticated figures like anticipation and the Charleston pattern, each with explicit practice rules (metronome for syncopation, phrase-by-phrase learning for repertoire). Load-bearing terminology introduced includes red dot notation for root, chord tone numbers for quality designation, anticipation (placing next-bar's chord on the & of 4), the Freddie Green rhythm, and the Charleston—all of which establish the harmonic and rhythmic vocabulary for beginning jazz guitarists.

# Chapter 2 — Playing Jazz Standards

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

This chapter teaches five core jazz standards—Birks' Works, Summertime, Maiden Voyage, Blue Bossa, and Autumn Leaves—establishing depth-over-breadth as the method's priority and recordings as the ear-first principle that precedes fretboard work. The master's characteristic move is bridging rock/blues players into jazz vocabulary: minor pentatonic soloing over Summertime, syncopation discipline in bossa grooves, and key transposition as a transferable discipline across tunes that share harmonic families. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes turnaround substitutions (bVI-V-I, ii-V-I, biii-bVI/ii-V-I in minor blues), modal harmony and Dorian modes, ii-V-I progressions in both major and minor forms, syncopation in comping and soloing, and chord melody as the three-element technique of playing bass, chords, and melody simultaneously with melody confined to the upper strings.

# Chapter 3 — Jazz Guitar Arpeggios

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the foundational technique of jazz guitar arpeggios—broken chords played with plectrum and muted to prevent ringing, independent from chord shapes and used for soloing rather than accompaniment—by anchoring all arpeggio shapes to the ii-V-I progression and introducing five movable grips for major, minor, and dominant chords that correspond to the CAGED system. The chapter establishes three functional purposes for arpeggios in improvisation: outlining harmonic direction, enabling smoother voice leading, and adding color through substitutions. Master technique surfaces in the voice-leading rule (connecting arpeggios by proximity rather than restarting from roots) and the creative liberation principle that arpeggios can start on any note and be reordered at will. Key terminology introduced includes arpeggio patterns (5ths/4ths skipping, groups of three), movable shapes, approach notes (chromatic tension created by approaching from one fret below, resolving to chord tones), and enclosures (the bebop decoration of surrounding each chord tone with notes one fret above and below).

# Chapter 4 — Jazz Guitar Scales

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches the deployment of four scales—major blues, natural minor (Aeolian), harmonic minor, and melodic minor—each assigned to specific chords within the minor ii–V–I progression and other jazz contexts, with the governing principle that scale choice follows harmonic change (Joe Pass's "When the chords change, you change"). The master's characteristic move is to justify each scale's use through a single differentiating feature: the major blues scale's b3 as the "blue note," harmonic minor's raised 7th that generates V7(b9,b13) tensions over the altered dominant, and melodic minor's raised 7th as an expressive tension device over m7 chords. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes the major blues scale's avoidance-of-clash rule (its lack of a 7th allows use over both dominant and major seventh chords), the Aeolian mode as the identity of natural minor, the V7alt (altered dominant) as the harmonic context requiring tonic harmonic minor rather than the chord's root, and the deliberate-choice model for expressive scale deployment (treating raised intervals as context-dependent decisions rather than automatic inclusions).

# Chapter 5 — Jazz Guitar Licks

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Jazz Guitar Licks teaches the core pedagogical principle of extracting transferable concepts from learned licks rather than parroting them wholesale, anchored in key terminology including blue notes (b3 and b5 from the blues scale), the phrygian dominant scale (fifth mode of harmonic minor) for V7 chords, the 3 to 9 arpeggio (dim7 from the 3rd of any 7th chord), and tritone substitution mechanics (playing a 7th chord a tritone away while preserving the 3rd and 7th). The chapter establishes essential bebop techniques such as triads for chord outlining, a balance between diatonic and chromatic lines, the enclosure technique (surrounding a target note for tension and release), and side-stepping (alternating between minor pentatonic scales a half step apart for modern jazz texture). Charlie Christian emerges as the master whose characteristic moves—enclosures, lower neighbor tones after leaps, and the b3 to 3 to 5 riff mixing blue notes with diatonic intervals—recur throughout, while additional vocabulary includes the major blues scale (major pentatonic plus b3), diminished scale construction (alternating whole and half steps), double stops over pedal tones, three-note-per-string legato adapted from rock, and Joe Pass's signature chord-melody rhythm technique of splitting bass from treble with selective accent.

# Chapter 6 — Jazz Blues

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

This chapter teaches the harmonic and melodic foundations of jazz blues by defining the characteristic chord qualities (dominant 7 tonic and basic I7-IV7-V7 changes), historically significant substitutions (Count Basie's #IVdim7 and VI7b9, bebop ii-V chains, tritone substitutions), and distinctive voice-leading techniques (contrary motion, sus chords for rhythmic tension). The load-bearing terminology includes quick change blues progression, Bird blues (defined by its Imaj7 tonic), tritone substitution, and the scale-mixing strategies that produce the jazz-blues sound: b3 and 3 mixing (minor blues + Mixolydian), Lydian Dominant over tritone substitutes, and harmonic minor over dominant 7b9 chords. The chapter highlights two master approaches—Kenny Burrell's chord/single-note mixing technique and Joe Pass's minor/major pentatonic mixing—while emphasizing that blues is defined not only by harmonic structure but by blue notes and sound/feeling, anchored in three structured solo practice methods that progress from imitation through composition to ear training.

# Chapter 7 — Appendix - The Roman Numeral System

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

This appendix introduces the Roman numeral system for labeling chord function in jazz, establishing capital Roman numerals (I, V, V7) for major and dominant chords and small Roman numerals (ii, iii, vi, vii°) for minor and diminished/half-diminished chords—a notational convention that distinguishes chord labeling from Arabic scale-degree numbering. The system extends beyond diatonic harmony to accommodate chromatic alterations and substitutions like tritone substitutions, marked with flats and sharps when chords fall outside the basic key center. The key load-bearing terminology introduced are Roman numeral chord labels themselves, along with the distinction between capital and small numeral notation as an encoding of chord quality that readers must internalize to interpret chord charts throughout the book. This is a reference appendix rather than an instructional chapter.

# Chapter 1 — Jazz Guitar Chords

_Pages 8-28._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Essential chord types

> The 17 chord shapes shown on the following pages are essential
> knowledge for every beginning jazz guitarist to get started. The chords
> you'll learn here are maj7, dominant 7, minor 7, half diminished 7 and
> diminished 7.

**Significance (p. 10).** This passage defines the complete set of chord qualities the chapter covers, establishing the harmonic vocabulary a beginning jazz guitarist must master.

## Root dot notation

> A red dot @ represents the root (aka 1 or bass note) of the chord. The root is
> the note that gives the chord the first part of its name (C or A for example). On
> this chart, all bass notes are C. By moving the chord shapes up or down the
> guitar neck you get other chords of the same type (you'll learn how a bit further,
> in the chapter about transposing chords).

**Significance (p. 10).** This defines the diagram notation system used throughout the entire book, making it foundational for reading every chord diagram that follows.

## Chord tone numbers

> The numbers (@@@_) inside the note markers are chord tones. These chord
> tones give the chord the second part of its name (maj7 or min7 for example).

**Significance (p. 10).** This explains how the numeric labels inside chord diagrams map to chord quality names, which is essential for understanding the notation throughout the method.

## String 6 root: right-hand technique

> The bass note is played with the
> thumb of the right hand, the other chord tones are simultaneously played with
> fingers 1, 2 and 3 (index, middle finger and ring finger).

**Significance (p. 10).** This prescribes the specific fingerpicking technique required for the root-on-string-6 chord voicings, establishing the right-hand approach used throughout this section.

## Transposing by moving root

> When doing so, you don't have to learn new chords, you
> just have to recognize where the root of the chord is and move it to the note you
> need.

**Significance (p. 13).** This states the core transposition rule of the method — that chord shapes are moveable and rooted, which unlocks all 12 keys from a single shape.

## Transposition rule: root = chord name

> Remember: the red note (in this case the lowest note), tells you what the
> name of the chord is when played on the guitar.

**Significance (p. 15).** This is the single governing rule for using moveable chord shapes across the neck, and the book explicitly flags it as a summary principle to memorize.

## ii V I vi progression

> you're ready to learn your first jazz chord
> progression: ii V | vi (if you’re not sure what these Roman numerals mean, click
> here to learn more about them in the appendix of this ebook).
>
> These chords, or variations of these chords, are found in countless jazz standards
> and are essential knowledge for anyone learning jazz guitar.
>
> The progression is taken from the major scale, where four of the seven chords
> are extracted and arranged to form this progression.

**Significance (p. 15).** This introduces the ii–V–I–vi as the foundational jazz progression, explaining its origin in the major scale and its ubiquity in jazz standards.

## Freddie Green rhythm

> The first rhythm (steady quarter notes
> aka four to the bar), is often called the
> Freddie Green rhythm, named after
> Count Basie’s guitarist. When playing this
> rhythm, accenting the 2nd and 4th notes
> of each bar gives you a swing feel that
> emulates Green's driving pulse.

**Significance (p. 19).** This names and defines the foundational jazz comping rhythm, and gives the specific instruction to accent beats 2 and 4 to achieve authentic swing feel.

## Beats 1 and 3: chord cutoff rule

> Go slow, count, and make sure you cut the chords off so they don't ring over on
> beats 2 and 4, which is important to maintaining the swing feel with this rhythm.

**Significance (p. 20).** This states an explicit rule about note duration — chords must be muted before beats 2 and 4 — which is a technique requirement, not just a stylistic preference.

## Beats 2 and 4 comping

> You now take the first and third beats out of each bar as you play chords on beats
> 2 and 4 in each measure.
>
> When doing so, you line up with the hi-hat on the drum kit, bringing a heavier
> swing to your comping in the process.

**Significance (p. 21).** This explains the rhythmic rationale for the 2-and-4 pattern by linking it to the drummer's hi-hat, teaching the guitarist how their comping fits the ensemble texture.

## Syncopation: rushing warning

> Though it sounds cool, it's hard to play syncopated rhythms and not rush the
> time. Because of this, work with a metronome and go slow until you're ready to
> speed this pattern.

**Significance (p. 22).** This gives an explicit practice rule — always use a metronome for syncopated figures — addressing the most common technical pitfall of this rhythm type.

## Anticipation defined

> The next rhythm places chords on the & of 2 and 4 in each bar. This technique is
> called “anticipation,” and is an essential jazz rhythmic concept.
>
> Anticipation is placing the chord from the next bar on the & of 4 in the bar
> you're on (such as the G7 on the & of 4 in bar 1).

**Significance (p. 22).** This defines anticipation — one of jazz's most characteristic comping devices — and provides a concrete example of how the next-bar chord is borrowed into the current bar.

## Charleston rhythm

> The final rhythm is called the Charleston, as it’s named after the famous dance
> from the early 1900s.
>
> In this rhythm, you play a chord on beat one of each bar, followed by a chord on
> the & of 2 in each bar.

**Significance (p. 23).** This names and defines the Charleston pattern — a cornerstone jazz comping figure — and specifies its two-attack placement (beat 1 and the & of 2).

## Autumn Leaves study: practice method

> Go slow, learn each four-bar phrase one at a time, and then put everything
> together from there as you learn the whole tune.
>
> After you can play these chords and rhythms, change the rhythms, as you expand
> this exercise in the woodshed.

**Significance (p. 24).** This prescribes a phrase-by-phrase learning sequence and then calls for rhythmic variation as an expansion exercise, modeling the method's broader approach to learning repertoire.

# Chapter 2 — Playing Jazz Standards

_Pages 29-47._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Learning jazz standards priority

> When learning these songs, you want to learn the melody, chords, and soloing, so
> you can function in a jam situation.
>
> Don't worry about learning all 10 at once, it’s better to have one that you can jam
> with other people, rather than have parts of several tunes under your fingers.

**Significance (p. 29).** This sets the method's core priority: depth on one tune over breadth across many, and functional jam-readiness as the measure of progress.

## Learning from recordings rule

> you can learn these songs from a Real Book, but make sure to listen to
> recorded versions by famous jazz musicians of these same songs.
>
> This introduces these songs and their interpretation to your ears, making it
> easier to learn when you take the chords, melody, and soloing to the fretboard.

**Significance (p. 29).** This establishes the method’s ear-first principle: recordings precede fretboard work so that interpretation is internalized before notes are placed.

## Minor blues turnaround variations

> As with any minor blues head, the turnaround for Birks’ Works has a few common
> variations that you will run into on a jam session. These variations include bVI-V-I,
> ii-V-I, or even biii-bVI/ii V/I.

**Significance (p. 30).** This defines the three standard turnaround substitution families a guitarist must recognize on sight in minor blues contexts.

## Summertime minor-key progressions

> It also acts as a nice introduction to common minor key progressions, such as ii V

**Significance (p. 33).** This identifies two transferable harmonic concepts — minor ii-V-I and tonic minor/major mixing — using Summertime as the teaching vehicle.

## Pentatonic as bridge to jazz soloing

> for beginner jazz guitarists, you can use the minor pentatonic or minor blues
> scale to solo over this entire progression. This makes it a great “bridge” tune for those
> coming from a rock and blues background and who are now studying jazz in their
> practice routine.

**Significance (p. 33).** This gives a concrete soloing rule — minor pentatonic/blues scale works over Summertime’s whole progression — explicitly bridging rock/blues vocabulary into a jazz context.

## Maiden Voyage modal harmony

> Either way, the chords are modal in nature, and move around to four different
> keys as you navigate this 32-bar form.
>
> A fun study in modal jazz, Dorian modes, rhythmic control, and key changes that
> challenges you in the woodshed and adds to your jam session Set list.

**Significance (p. 34).** This defines Maiden Voyage’s harmonic identity as a Dorian modal study with four key centers, framing it as the entry point for modal jazz vocabulary.

## Blue Bossa syncopation rule

> Because it’s a bossa groove, you need to spend time on syncopation in both your
> soloing and comping.
>
> Working on syncopation in your chords and lines not only prepares you to jam
> this tune, it prepares you for many other jazz jam situations as well.

**Significance (p. 38).** This gives a genre-specific rule — bossa requires syncopation in both comping and soloing — and generalizes it as a skill that transfers beyond this single tune.

## Autumn Leaves key duality

> It can be played in Em or Gm, and therefore is an introduction to tunes that are
> commonly played in more than one key, forcing you to learn it in both keys.
>
> There are also major ii V I’s and minor ii V I's to navigate in your comping and
> soloing, and the melody line is ripe for a chord melody arrangement.

**Significance (p. 39).** This defines Autumn Leaves as the canonical study for both key-transposition discipline and mixed major/minor ii-V-I navigation in a single form.

## Chord melody melody placement

> The melody has been moved to mostly the top 2 strings, compared to the whole
> fretboard in the single-note example. This is to accommodate the chords played
> below the melody line in this arrangement.

**Significance (p. 43).** This states the fundamental voicing principle of chord melody: melody is confined to the upper strings so chord harmony can be voiced beneath it.

## Chord melody definition

> Playing bass, chords and melody at the same time (aka chord melody) is not
> an easy thing to do.

**Significance (p. 47).** This provides the book’s explicit three-element definition of chord melody — bass, chords, and melody simultaneously — as a distinct technique category.

# Chapter 3 — Jazz Guitar Arpeggios

_Pages 48-82._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Arpeggio definition

> An arpeggio is a broken chord, where the notes of the chord are played in
> succession instead of simultaneously.

**Significance (p. 48).** This is the foundational definition the entire chapter builds on, distinguishing arpeggios from strummed chords.

## Jazz vs. pop arpeggio technique

> In jazz (and blues, metal, etc), arpeggios are used for soloing instead of
> accompaniment. In contrast to arpeggios used in other genres of music, the
> notes of a jazz guitar arpeggio are usually played with a plectrum (unless you play
> fingerstyle) and muted after they are played, so they don't ring together. Another
> contrast is that these arpeggios are not based on a chord shape.

**Significance (p. 49).** This defines the jazz-specific execution technique—plectrum, note muting, and independence from chord shapes—that separates jazz arpeggio practice from classical or pop usage.

## Arpeggios in soloing: three uses

> ¢ Playing the chord tones in your guitar solo will outline the harmony of the tune.
> This gives your improvisation a sense of direction, making it more interesting to
> listen to.
>
> ¢ Arpeggios make it easier to improvise a nice voice leading, making your solos
> more melodic.
>
> * You can use arpeggios to add color and complexity to your solos by using
> substitutions.

**Significance (p. 50).** This enumerates the three functional reasons for using arpeggios in jazz improvisation, framing the chapter's practical goals.

## Scope of arpeggio knowledge required

> Every jazz guitarists needs to know how to play the arpeggios of all chord
> types in all positions of the guitar neck.

**Significance (p. 50).** This is the method's core prescriptive rule establishing the full scope of study expected from the student.

## 251 progression arpeggio types

> We're going to learn the basic arpeggio shapes (aka grips) by looking at the most
> common chord progression in jazz, the 251 (II V1).
>
> In this example we'll be working with a2 5 1 progression in the key of G major

**Significance (p. 51).** This anchors all subsequent arpeggio shapes to the ii-V-I context, explaining why minor, dominant, and major are the three essential types to learn first.

## Root dot notation convention

> © red dots represent the root or 1 of the guitar chord. The letter inside the box
> is the note name.
> LD} black dots represent the other chord notes.

**Significance (p. 52).** This defines the diagram notation system used throughout all the arpeggio charts in the chapter.

## Arpeggio Pattern #1: 5ths/4ths by skipping

> Am7 Arpeggio Pattern #1: This first pattern plays the arpeggio in 5th and 4th
> intervals, achieved by skipping notes:

**Significance (p. 54).** This introduces interval skipping as an advanced variation that develops non-linear arpeggio fluency.

## Arpeggio Pattern #2: groups of three

> Am7 Arpeggio Pattern #2: this pattern divides the arpeggio in groups of 3 notes:

**Significance (p. 55).** This introduces rhythmic grouping as a bebop displacement device across the beat.

## Movable arpeggio shapes

> One thing you need to know: just like chords, all arpeggio shapes are
> movable. If you know the arpeggio for Am7 you can use that same

**Significance (p. 59).** This establishes the transposition principle that allows one learned shape to generate arpeggios for any chord of the same type across the neck.

## Alternating arpeggios: voice leading rule

> The next step is alternating the arpeggios. We do this by playing the first arpeggio
> (Am7) for 1 bar and then switch to the nearest note of the second arpeggio (D7) in
> the second bar. The same happens when we switch to the third arpeggio (Gmaj7).

**Significance (p. 61).** This defines the voice-leading principle of connecting arpeggios by proximity rather than restarting each from the root, the key to musical-sounding sequences.

## Creative arpeggio improvisation rule

> Arpeggios can be started on any note and played in any order. You can
> mix notes, skip notes and use any rhythm you can think of. Be creative!

**Significance (p. 62).** This rule liberates the student from mechanical root-to-root patterns and is the conceptual bridge between exercise and improvisation.

## CAGED system arpeggio link

> Those of you familiar with the CAGED system, will recognize that the 5 Gmaj7
> arpeggio shapes above correspond with the 5 basic chord shapes (CAGE D):

**Significance (p. 66).** This cross-references the five arpeggio grips to the CAGED system, allowing prior chord-shape knowledge to scaffold arpeggio learning.

## Diminished chord symmetry

> You only need to learn 2 grips for diminished chords because diminished chords
> are symmetrical (learn more about diminished chords here).

**Significance (p. 70).** This gives a specific rule reducing the memorization burden for diminished arpeggios, grounded in the chord's symmetrical structure.

## Approach notes technique

> The first arpeggio concept is called approach notes, where you approach
> any note in an arpeggio by one fret below. When doing so, you create a
> tension and release sound in your lines.
>
> The only rule is that you can’t resolve to the chromatic notes. So, if you play an
> approach note, you then have to play a chord tone afterwards.

**Significance (p. 74).** This defines the approach-note technique with both its execution instruction and its governing rule, making it the complete practical unit for applying chromatic color to arpeggios.

## Enclosures technique

> The next bebop technique uses two chromatic notes for each arpeggio note, as
> you encircle chord tones in your lines.
>
> When playing enclosures, you play one fret above, then one fret below,
> then the chord tone.

**Significance (p. 76).** This defines the enclosure technique—the most common bebop chromatic decoration—with a precise three-step execution sequence.

# Chapter 4 — Jazz Guitar Scales

_Pages 83-95._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Major Blues Scale application

> The first scale is used to solo over the first four bars of Autumn Leaves, Am7-D7-
> Gmaj7-Cmaj7, as all chords are in the key of G major.

**Significance (p. 83).** This establishes the explicit chord-context rule for deploying the major blues scale, grounding the scale in its functional harmonic home.

## Major Blues Scale blue note

> The b3 is the “blue” note in this scale, as it gives the scale its bluesy sound.

**Significance (p. 83).** This identifies the single chromatic pitch that defines the character of the major blues scale and distinguishes it from the major pentatonic.

## Major Blues Scale versatility

> You can use this scale to solo over any progression in a major key, such as Autumn
> Leaves, or over individual 7th and maj7 chords. Because there is not 7th in this
> scale, it can be applied to both G7 and Gmaj7 chords.

**Significance (p. 83).** This gives the student an explicit avoidance-of-clash rule explaining why the scale is safe over both dominant and major seventh chords.

## Natural Minor over minor ii chord

> The next scale is used to solo over the F#m7b5 chord in Autumn Leaves.
>
> Because minor ii

**Significance (p. 85).** This introduces the shared-root strategy for navigating a minor ii–V–I, a fundamental organisational concept for jazz soloing that reduces fingering complexity.

## Natural Minor as Aeolian mode

> This scale is the 6th mode of the major scale, and is also called the Aeolian mode:

**Significance (p. 85).** This cross-references the natural minor scale to its modal identity, giving students a conceptual bridge to broader modal vocabulary.

## Harmonic Minor over V7alt

> When soloing over the V7alt chord (the B7alt chord in Autumn Leaves) in a minor
> ii V I, you use the tonic harmonic minor scale.

**Significance (p. 87).** This states the core rule for handling the altered dominant in a minor ii–V–I, directing the student to the tonic harmonic minor rather than the chord’s own root.

## Harmonic Minor raised 7th distinction

> Here are the notes of the harmonic minor scale. Note that the only difference
> with the natural minor scale is the natural 7 instead of a flat 7:

**Significance (p. 87).** This defines the harmonic minor by its single differentiating interval, making it easy for students to derive it from the already-learned natural minor.

## Harmonic Minor V7 sound

> When playing the E harmonic minor scal over B7alt, you create a V7(b9,b13)
> sound:

**Significance (p. 87).** This names the specific altered tensions produced when harmonic minor is superimposed over the V7alt chord, connecting scale choice to chord-tone vocabulary.

## Melodic Minor over Im7

> The final scale is used to solo over the Im7 chord in a minor ii V |, melodic minor.
>
> This scale has a raised 7th interval, which creates tension over m7 chords in your
> solos:

**Significance (p. 89).** This assigns melodic minor its harmonic function in the minor ii–V–I and flags the raised 7th as an expressive tension device rather than a neutral colour tone.

## Melodic Minor expressive use

> Because of this tension, experiment with this scale in your solos to determine
> where you want to use that raised 7th in your lines.

**Significance (p. 90).** This gives a deliberate-choice rule, instructing students to treat the raised 7th as a context-dependent decision rather than an automatic inclusion.

## Scale-change rule (Joe Pass)

> “When the chords change, you change”

**Significance (p. 95).** This aphorism encapsulates the chapter’s governing principle—that scale selection is driven by chord-by-chord harmonic context—and serves as the conceptual capstone of the entire section.

# Chapter 5 — Jazz Guitar Licks

_Pages 96-156._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Avoiding "line player" trap

> you learn lines, break them down into concepts, and then use those
> concepts to build exercises and further vocabulary in your playing.

**Significance (p. 96).** This sentence states the method's core pedagogical rule — the correct way to use licks is to extract transferable concepts, not to parrot them wholesale.

## Blue notes definition

> These chromatic notes are known
> as blue notes (b3 and b5) and come from the blues scale, which gives the lick it’s
> bluesy vibe.

**Significance (p. 98).** Defines the term "blue notes" with specific interval identities (b3 and b5) and links them to the blues scale, grounding a recurring concept used throughout the chapter.

## Triads in bebop soloing

> Though arpeggios are mostly used in jazz to outline chords, swing and bebop era
> players often used the 1-3-5 triad to outline the underlying chord. When working
> on soloing over bebop changes, don't forget to spend some time on triads, they'll
> come in handy as you use them over bebop tunes.

**Significance (p. 99).** Gives a prescriptive rule — practice triads for bebop soloing — and explains the historical practice that justifies it.

## Phrygian dominant over V7

> The scale actually begins in the second half of the first bar, on the note A, and
> uses the fifth mode of harmonic minor to create a typical bebop run over the
> next 6 beats. When soloing in the bebop style, the phrygian dominant scale is a
> first-choice sound when improvising over V7 and V7alt chords in your lines.

**Significance (p. 101).** Identifies the phrygian dominant scale (fifth mode of harmonic minor) as a primary, rule-level choice over V7 and V7alt chords in the bebop idiom.

## Diatonic vs. chromatic lines

> When playing over bebop tunes, you don’t always have to use chromatic notes to
> outline the changes. Sometimes a carefully played diatonic run, such as this, is
> exactly what the tune needs at that moment in time.
>
> Having a handful of diatonic lines in your vocabulary will ensure you're able to
> mix them in comfortably with the chromatic lines in your repertoire.

**Significance (p. 102).** Establishes a balance rule — maintain both diatonic and chromatic lines — countering the assumption that bebop always requires chromaticism.

## 3 to 9 arpeggio (dim7 from 3rd)

> When playing a dim7 arpeggio from the 3rd of any 7th chord, you'll outline the
> 3-5-b7-b9 intervals of that chord:
>
> eee ee
>
> eo
>
> This is called a 3 to 9 arpeggio, an essential learning for any bebop guitarist.

**Significance (p. 104).** Defines the "3 to 9 arpeggio" concept with its precise intervallic content, naming it as an essential bebop technique applicable to any 7th chord.

## Tritone substitution mechanics

> The basic application of a tritone sub is to take any 7th chord you see and
> play another 7th chord that occurs a tritone (#4 aka b5) away from that
> initial chord.
>
> For example: playing F#7 over C7. The reason that this sub works is that 7th
> chords with a bass note a tritone apart share the same 3rd and 7th:
>
> on | ee
>
> As you can see:
>
> ¢ The 3rd of C7 (E) is the same as the b7 of F#7 (E).
> ¢ The b7 of C7 (Bb) is the same as the 3rd of F#7 (A#=Bb).
>
> This is glue that holds the two tritone sub chords together.

**Significance (p. 105).** Provides a complete, interval-level explanation of why the tritone substitution works, making it a self-contained theory definition with a concrete example.

## Major blues scale definition

> Using the major blues scale (the major pentatonic scale with an added b3) is
> a great way to bring a Christian and swing sound to your lines, so feel free to
> explore this idea further as you take it past the context of this single lick in your
> practice routine.

**Significance (p. 117).** Defines the major blues scale by its formula (major pentatonic + b3) and assigns it a stylistic context (Christian/swing sound), making it a reusable concept.

## Enclosure technique

> Here you see an enclosure over the 3rd of the underlying A7 chord, where D and
> C and being used to enclose the note C. This type of tension and release line (aka
> enclosure) is something that can be found in many of Charlie's and other solos
> from the Swing era.
>
> Enclosures have been used by just about every great jazz soloist over the
> past 80 or so years, and so it is an important concept to have under your fingers
> and in your ears as you advance your jazz guitar skills.

**Significance (p. 118).** Names and defines the enclosure technique, explains its structural logic (surrounding a target note), and establishes it as a universal jazz vocabulary item.

## Lower neighbor tone after leap

> By landing on a chromatic note after a leap, before resolving it to a chord tone on
> the next note, you can bring a strong focus to both the leap and the resolution
> point in your lines, something that Charlie and other swing musicians liked to do
> in their solos.

**Significance (p. 119).** Articulates the lower-neighbor-tone rule as a general melodic technique: use a chromatic passing note after a large interval to intensify both the leap and its resolution.

## b3 to 3 to 5 riff (blue note mixing)

> This Charlie Christian lick uses an idiomatic riff that runs from the b3 to the 3
> to the 5th of the underlying chord, in this case G7 and Cmaj7. Mixing the blue
> note (b3) with the diatonic 3rd and 5th of any chord is something that Charlie and
> many other Swing artists like to do in their soloing lines and phrases.

**Significance (p. 120).** Describes a reusable intervallic pattern (b3–3–5) as a generalizable idiomatic move applicable across chords, not just the one lick example.

## Diminished scale over dominant

> To create a
> diminished scale, alternate between whole steps and half steps from the root on.

**Significance (p. 124).** Defines the diminished scale by construction (alternating W-H steps), explains its symmetrical property, and gives the key rule for using it over dominants (root a half step above).

## Side-stepping technique

> This lick is built by playing the A minor pentatonic in bar one, the Bb minor
> pentatonic in bar two, before resolving back to the A minor pentatonic in bar
> three.
>
> Playing between two minor pentatonic scales over a 7th chord, a 1/2 step
> apart, is a fun and cool way to bring a modern vibe to your jazz blues solos.

**Significance (p. 114).** Defines side-stepping as a concrete, reproducible technique — alternating between two pentatonic scales a half step apart — and identifies it as a key component of modern jazz vocabulary.

## Double stops definition

> Besides playing single-note soloing ideas in a jazz blues context, you can also
> expand your soloing by exploring double stops.
>
> A double stop means playing 2 notes at once.
>
> This phrase is built from the A mixolydian mode, the mode most associated with
> the dominant 7th chord sound, and uses double stops on top of an A pedal. This
>
> technique, playing double stops on top of a root note, is common practice and so
> is worth exploring further.

**Significance (p. 112).** Defines double stops, links the mixolydian mode to the dominant 7th chord, and introduces the pedal-tone + double-stop technique as a transferable device.

## Three-note-per-string legato

> Though many of us associate three-note-per-string scales with Rock and Metal,
> Pat translates these scale shapes to the jazz idiom as he uses to hammer-ons per
> string to build a highly fluid line over a Dm7 chord.
>
> If you like this approach, go back and work on three-note-per-string scales and
> add as many slurs per string as you can in order to digest this side of Pat’s
> soloing vocabulary.

**Significance (p. 139).** Transfers the three-note-per-string/legato technique from rock to jazz, providing a concrete practice prescription (maximize slurs per string) to develop fluid phrasing.

## Joe Pass chord-melody rhythm technique

> The
> idea is that you break up the chord into the bass note and the top 3 notes of the
> shape, alternating back and forth until you get to the chromatic approach notes
> in the last two 8th notes of each bar. Try accenting the chords only, not the bass
> notes, to bring an added Joe sound to the mix with this line.

**Significance (p. 128).** Describes Joe Pass's idiomatic chord-melody technique — splitting bass from treble and accenting only the chord portion — as a reproducible rhythmic device for solo guitar.

# Chapter 6 — Jazz Blues

_Pages 157-191._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Blues characteristics

> The tonic chord of a blues is a dominant 7 chord, a fact that doesn't fit very well
> in traditional music theory.
>
> ¢ The blues is not only about chord changes and scales, but is also about
> a certain sound, a feeling. Responsible for that sound are the blue
> notes: a lowered 3rd note and a lowered 5th note.
>
> * The 3 basic chords of a blues are all dominant 7 chords.

**Significance (p. 158).** Defines the core harmonic and tonal properties that distinguish blues from standard tonal music.

## Lead sheet chord embellishment rule

> those chords can be embellished with 9ths,
> 13ths, 6ths, and other color tones. So, if you hear or see a G13 chord and it's
> written as G7, that's a common approach to comping over lead sheet jazz-blues
> chord changes

**Significance (p. 159).** Practical comping rule teaching players how to read jazz lead sheets and expand basic chord symbols with color tones.

## I-IV-V blues form

> Notice
> how these changes use only three chords, the |7, V7 and V7 chords. Because of
> this, they are often referred to as I-IV-V blues chord changes.

**Significance (p. 160).** Defines the foundational terminology for the basic 12-bar blues form against which all later progressions are measured.

## Quick change blues progression

> Because there is a quick move to the IV7 chord and back to the tonic in the
> first three bars, this chord progression is often referred to as a quick change
> blues progression.

**Significance (p. 162).** Defines 'quick change blues' and the harmonic move (IV7 in bar 2) that distinguishes the 1930s form from basic blues.

## Count Basie blues innovations

> One of the innovations Count Basie brought to the blues, or at least popularized,
> is the use of the #IVdim7 chord in bars two and 6 of a jazz blues progression.
>
> You will also see in the examples below that there is a iim7-V7/IV in bar 4 of the
> tune, as well as a VI7b9 chord in bar 8, both now commonly used ideas that were
> popularized by the Basie Band.

**Significance (p. 164).** Identifies the specific harmonic substitutions (#IVdim7, iim7-V7/IV, VI7b9) associated with the Basie blues style.

## Bebop blues ii-V moves

> Getting into the bebop era with these changes, you will notice a ii-V of the iim7
> chord in bar 8, as well as a iii-VI-ii-V progression in the last bar of the tune, which
> showcases the bebopper’s love of ii-V’s and fast moving changes.

**Significance (p. 167).** Defines structural hallmarks of bebop blues harmony — chain ii-V substitutions and turnaround cycles.

## Tritone substitution blues

> * Bar 6: the Bb7 is replaced with a tritone ii-V (Bm7-E7).
> ¢ Bar 6 and 7: there are four descending 7th chords in bars 7 and 8, with the E7 and
>
> Eb7 being used to connect F7 and D7b9 chromatically.
> * Bar 10: there is a tritone approach chord added to the Am7, Bb7 in place of E7
> (the V7 of Am7).

**Significance (p. 169).** Bar-by-bar instructions for applying tritone substitution to specific locations in the blues form.

## Bird Blues form characteristics

> Reflecting the bebop love of ii-V's, this progression is full of various ii-V progressions
> in anumber of different keys.
>
> A Bird blues starts and ends with an Fmaj7 chord, which is odd for a blues
> progression.

**Significance (p. 172).** Defines the defining feature of Bird Blues — the Imaj7 tonic replacing the usual I7.

## Contrary motion voice leading

> Bar 5-6: there’s some contrary motion going on here. The bass goes from c to c#
> (upward motion), while the d of C9 goes to the c# (downward motion) of C#°7.
> Contrary motion in voice leading sounds nice.

**Significance (p. 178).** Concrete bar-specific example of contrary-motion voice leading stated as a preferred technique.

## Sus chord comping technique

> Bar 10: Sus chords are a nice way to delay and bring extra motion to dominant
> chords.

**Significance (p. 178).** Concise rule explaining sus chords' functional role in jazz comping as a rhythmic/harmonic tension device.

## Mixing b3 and 3 for bluesy sound

> The first 8 bars use the F minor pentatonic scale, mixed with the major 3rd (bar
>
> 3). Mixing b3 and 3 is often used by jazz musicians to create a bluesy sound. One
>
> way to do this is mixing the F minor blues scale with the F Mixolydian scale.

**Significance (p. 182).** Names the scale-mixing strategy (minor blues + Mixolydian) that generates the characteristic jazz-blues sound.

## Lydian Dominant as tritone sub scale

> Bar 16 uses the B Lydian Dominant scale (= F Altered scale). B7 is the tritone
> substitute of F7 and creates an altered sound over F.

**Significance (p. 183).** Defines Lydian Dominant's function as the scale choice over a tritone substitute chord, linking substitution theory to scale selection.

## Harmonic minor for 7b9 sound

> The classic lick in bar 20 uses the G Harmonic Minor scale over D7, creating a 7b9
> sound:

**Significance (p. 184).** States the rule for using harmonic minor over a dominant chord to generate the 7b9 color.

## Kenny Burrell chord/single-note mixing

> one of the key elements to explore is mixing chords and single-
> lines during each phrase of an improvised chorus, which you can hear during the
> melody section of Chitlins especially.

**Significance (p. 185).** Defines the characteristic Burrell technique — interspersing three-note chord shapes with blues-scale single lines.

## Solo practice methods

> 1. Play the solo with a backing track over the first chorus. During the second
> chorus you improvise the single lines but keep the chords as is. Continue this
> alteration throughout the backing track.
>
> 2. Write out your own Kenny Burrell inspired solo over an A minor blues
> progression, using the blues scale for the single notes, and small, three-note
> chords for the comping sections of the solo.
>
> 3. Practice singing an improvised single-line where you see the single-notes in
> the solo above, then comp the chords on the guitar where they land. This is a
> great way to connect your ears and voice to your fretboard while working ona
> minor blues at the same time.

**Significance (p. 186).** Three structured practice protocols for internalizing chord-melody soloing, progressing from imitation to composition to ear-training.

## Joe Pass minor/major pentatonic mixing

> Here is another example of a jazz blues solo (over a blues in G), this time in the
> style of Joe Pass. In this example he mixes the minor and major pentatonic scales.

**Significance (p. 188).** Identifies the defining technique of the Joe Pass solo example — minor/major pentatonic mixing.

# Chapter 7 — Appendix - The Roman Numeral System

_Pages 192-197._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Roman Numeral chord labeling

> When talking about chord function in jazz we use Roman Numerals to label
> each chord in a given key. We do this to differentiate chord numbers from scale
> numbers (which are written with Arabic numerals such as 1, 2 and 3).

**Significance (p. 192).** Defines the foundational purpose of the Roman numeral system and the distinction from Arabic scale-degree numbering.

## Capital vs. small Roman Numerals

> * Capital Roman Numerals (Imaj7, \Vmaj7, V7): are used to indicate Major and
> Dominant chords.
>
> ¢ Small Roman Numerals (iim7, iiim7, vimm7, viimm7b5): are used to indicate
> Minor and Diminished/Half-Diminished chords.

**Significance (p. 192).** Core notational rule: capitalization encodes chord quality, required to interpret any Roman-numeral chord chart in the book.

## Chromatic alterations outside key

> If the chord appears outside of the basic key center (a tritone substitution for
> example) b’s and #'s are used to indicate these chords.

**Significance (p. 193).** Extends the Roman numeral system beyond diatonic harmony to cover chromatic substitutions like tritone subs.
